steps: Every increase of 2 in \(a\) multiplies \(f(a)\) by the constant \(c\). All four choices are equivalent forms of the same function; verify with choice C: \(f(x)=48(64)^x\). When \(x\) increases by 2, \(f(x+2)=48(64)^{x+2}=48(64)^x \cdot 64^2 = f(x)\cdot 4096\). So \(c=4096\).
The question asks which form displays \(c\) as the base or coefficient. Since \(c=4096\), we need a form where 4096 appears literally as the base of the exponential or as a coefficient.

Check each choice for whether its base or coefficient equals 4096:
  • A: \(f(x)=48(2)^{6x}\) — base is 2, not 4096. When \(x\) increases by 2, factor is \(2^{12}=4096\), but 4096 is not visible as the base or coefficient. Eliminated.
  • B: \(f(x)=48(8)^{2x}\) — base is 8, not 4096. Factor per \(+2\) is \(8^4=4096\), but 4096 is not displayed. Eliminated.
  • C: \(f(x)=48(64)^x\) — base is 64, not 4096. Factor per \(+2\) is \(64^2=4096\), but 4096 is not displayed. Eliminated.
  • D: \(f(x)=48(4096)^{\frac{x}{2}}\) — base is 4096. When \(x\) increases by 2, \(\frac{x}{2}\) increases by 1, so \(f\) multiplies by \(4096^1=4096=c\). The value of \(c\) is the base itself. That matches choice D — D is correct.
